content.problemStatement:
Alice has an array AAA of length NNN. She is interested in finding the number of pairs (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that 1≤i<j≤N1\leq i \lt j \leq N1≤i<j≤N and Ai⊕Aj<Ai&AjA_i \oplus A_j \lt A_i \& A_jAi​⊕Aj​<Ai​&Aj​.

⊕\oplus⊕ represents the Bitwise XOR operator.
&\&& represents the Bitwise AND operator.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain an integer TTT — number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains two lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN, the length of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots , A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, output the number of pairs (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that 1≤i<j≤N1 \leq i \lt j \leq N1≤i<j≤N and Ai⊕Aj<Ai&AjA_i \oplus A_j \lt A_i \& A_jAi​⊕Aj​<Ai​&Aj​.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of pairs (i, j) (1 ≤ i < j ≤ N) such that Ai XOR Aj < Ai AND Aj.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Numbers with msb 0: two 1's → 1 pair. Numbers with msb 1: 2 and 3 → 1 pair. Total 2 pairs satisfy the condition.
Only the numbers 2 (10) and 3 (11) share the same most significant bit (position 1). Hence exactly one valid pair.
All five numbers are 1 (msb position 0). Number of pairs = C(5,2) = 10.

solution.approach: For two positive integers x and y, let msb(x) be the index of the most‑significant set bit (0‑based). If msb(x)=msb(y) then the highest bit is 1 in both numbers, so (x AND y) has that bit set while (x XOR y) has it cleared, guaranteeing (x XOR y) < (x AND y). If the msb positions differ, the higher msb belongs to exactly one of the numbers, making that bit 1 in the XOR and 0 in the AND, so the inequality fails. Hence a pair is valid iff both numbers share the same msb. The solution counts how many numbers have each possible msb (0…30) and adds C(cnt,2) for each group.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (single pass to count msb frequencies). Overall O(total N) across all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra – an array of size 31 to store frequencies.

code.C:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){int T; if(scanf("%d",&T)!=1) return 0; while(T--){int N; scanf("%d",&N); long long cnt[31]={0}; for(int i=0;i<N;i++){unsigned int x; scanf("%u",&x); int msb=31-__builtin_clz(x); cnt[msb]++; } long long ans=0; for(int k=0;k<31;k++) ans+=cnt[k]*(cnt[k]-1)/2; printf("%lld\n",ans);} return 0; }
code.Python3:
import sys

def msb(x):
    return x.bit_length()-1

data=sys.stdin.read().split()
if not data:
    sys.exit()
it=iter(data)
T=int(next(it))
out=[]
for _ in range(T):
    N=int(next(it))
    cnt=[0]*31
    for _ in range(N):
        x=int(next(it))
        cnt[msb(x)]+=1
    ans=sum(c*(c-1)//2 for c in cnt)
    out.append(str(ans))
print('\n'.join(out))
